retrieved_docs.title: Influenza Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Differently combined HA and NA antigens are seen in influenza A, which undergo antigenic drifts and shifts resulting in antigenic variation and, thereby, the necessity for vaccine strain types to vary accordingly. Antigenic drifts are genetic changes occurring in the virus due to various actions of polymerases leading to gradual antigenic changes in both HA and NA, producing new variant strains. An antigenic shift occurs when the currently circulating virus disappears and is replaced by a new subtype with novel glycoproteins, to which antibodies against the previously circulating subtype do not cross-react. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Antibody -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Once an antigen binds an antibody, isotype switching occurs in which the Ig most suited for eliminating that pathogen gets expressed on the surface of the B cell. Specificity increases by the concurrent processes of somatic hypermutation and affinity maturation. B cells continuously undergo mutations to increase the chance of producing a more specific antibody. B cell mutations with the highest affinity for the pathogen remain, while the ones with lower affinity get eliminated. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Best Practices For Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: A potential complication of human mAbs use against viral pathogens is that it may promote selection for escape mutants. [14] Many mAbs addressed this issue by using a combination of antibodies directed against non-overlapping epitopes. [14] However, as new variants and subvariants emerged, their efficacy against the SARS-CoV-2 virus dwindled to the point that they are no longer authorized for use.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Complementary-determining regions (CDRs) contained within each antigen-binding fragment (Fab) of each respective antibody play an essential role in determining the specificity and affinity with which antibodies bind their target antigens. These highly specific regions are why mAbs can be applied to target precise targets whilst limiting the effects on alternate systems. The Fc region is another region of the antibody that contains constant domains and acts to activate the immune system against the target of mAbs. These functions are mediated by the binding and activation of other Fc receptors expressed on endogenous cells and the complement system, leading to the activation of effector function. [1] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Antibody -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Antibodies are naturally produced by plasma cells within the human body to mediate an adaptive immune response against invading pathogens. There are five predominant antibodies produced, each specialized to execute certain functions. Antibodies gain the ability to identify a diverse range of antigens by genetic recombination of different elements of its structure and while the affinity for a specific antigen derives from affinity maturation and somatic recombination processes. Antibodies have a plethora of clinical applications, the most important being their use in combating autoimmune conditions and cancer, conferring passive immunity, and their diagnostic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A third dose (booster dose) has been included in the vaccination schedule of various nations, with studies showing some amount of waning of immunity after two doses and a third dose offering higher protection levels. [128] [129] A phase 2 randomized controlled trial from the United Kingdom, which compared various combinations of boosting regimens, concluded that mixing vaccine types boosted antibodies and neutralizing responses for all seven vaccines studied, including most major commercially available vaccines. [130] Preexposure Prophylaxis(PrEP) to prevent SARS-CoV-2 infection

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Antibody -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Antibodies recognize millions of different antigens due to the diversity generated during antibody synthesis. The heavy chain and light chains are subject to genetic recombination, in which different gene combinations occur to increase the diversity of the final gene product. Additionally, an enzyme called terminal deoxynucleotidyl inserts random nucleotides during the recombination process, increasing diversity. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Complement Cascade -- Related Testing -- Functional screening tests
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory testing for complement components includes tests for functional activity of the CP (CH50 and its equivalents), the AP (AH50 or APH50), and the MBL pathway. The CH50 has its basis on a hemolytic assay in which an immune complex is formed by adding antibodies that react with a surface antigen on sheep red blood cells. When the antigen-fixed antibodies activate complement on the cell surface, the cell lyses and hemoglobin is released. Since the formation of the MAC on the cell requires the sequential action of all nine components of the classical (C1, C4, and C2) and terminal (C3, C5, C6, C7, C8, and C9) pathways, titrating the complement source (serum in most cases) so that only a portion of the cells present undergo lysis, the amount of active complement can be calculated, and the results get expressed as the reciprocal of the serum dilution that caused lysis of 50% of the cells in the assay. Two variations of the CH50 are currently in use in clinical laboratories: an assay based on the lysis of liposomes that releases an enzyme that can be read on an analyzer of the sort used for other clinical laboratory tests and solid-phase assays (ELISAs) that detect the final C9 neoantigen that forms when the complete pathway becomes activated. The CH50 is the single best screen for complement abnormalities in that the absence or decrease of activity in the CH50 implies that at least one of the necessary components is missing or low. The analogous assay for AP activity, the AH50, is not as widely available as the CH50, but it is useful as a screen for complement deficiency, especially when used in conjunction with the CH50. The AH50 depends on the unique properties of erythrocytes from certain species to provide a surface that promotes activation of the AP, with sequential activation of factors D, B, P, C3, C5, C6, C7, C8, and C9. Properdin is necessary for the stabilization of the C3 convertase (C3bBb), and inefficient activation of the AH50 occurs if P is low or absent. The most common variation of the AH50 uses rabbit red cells in combination with a buffer that blocks the activation of the CP or LP. Like the CH50, the AH50 is a measure of the percentage of rabbit erythrocytes lysed by diluted serum and is expressed in units that represent the dilution that lyses 50% of the cells used in the assay. If both CH50 and AH50 are used to screen for complement deficiency, it minimizes the number of additional tests required to pinpoint the defect.

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management -- Anti-SARS-CoV-2 Neutralizing Antibody Products
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals recovering from COVID-19 develop neutralizing antibodies against SARS-CoV-2, and the duration of how long this immunity lasts is unclear. Nevertheless, their role as therapeutic agents in managing COVID-19 is extensively being pursued in clinical trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Antibody -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Antibodies can persist in monomeric (IgG, IgE, IgD), dimeric (IgA) or pentameric (IgM) isotypes. A single antibody consists of four polypeptides, two of which are the heavy chains and two light chains. The heavy and light chains come together, forming a “Y” shaped structure, and different parts of the structure mediate different functions. The fragment antigen-binding (Fab) region determines the antibody’s idiotype and has an affinity for pathogenic antigens which allows the antibody to mediate its effect once bound to the antigen. The Fragment crystallizable (Fc) region of antibodies carries out other immune-related functions, such as complement binding, macrophage binding, and determining isotopes of that antibody. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Antigenic variation is a mutation in proteins typically recognized by antibodies and lymphocytes. HIV continually mutates, making protection and vaccine development difficult. Virches disrupt the interferon response by disrupting 2',5'-oligoadenylate synthetase activity or producing soluble interferon receptors. Viruses affect the expression of MHC molecules by several mechanisms. They can also infect immune cells; normal T and B cells are also sites of virus persistence. HIV hides in CD4+T cells, and EBV in B cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease is organized hierarchically to include human vaccination, and human vaccination in turn encompasses التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Injection of RhD immune globulin represents a specific form of التمنيع السلبي, illustrating that this RhD immune‑globulin injection is an example of passive immunization used within the broader context of human vaccination strategies for autoimmune disease prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 881
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations represent a broad category of therapeutic interventions that encompass immunization, injections, and perfusion. Within this overarching group, human vaccination is a specific subclass, and it further includes distinct forms such as immunization for autoimmune disease and passive immunization (التمنيع السلبي). Passive immunization, in turn, comprises interventions like the injection of RhD immune globulin, which is recognized as a type of passive immunization. Additionally, prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified both as a therapeutic intervention (under immunization, injections, and perfusion) and as a component of the larger prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations category. This hierarchical organization highlights how various vaccination and immunization strategies are interrelated, ranging from general preventive measures to specialized applications for autoimmune conditions and passive immune protection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease includes human vaccination as a subordinate concept, indicating that human vaccination is regarded as a specific form of immunization employed to prevent or modulate autoimmune disease in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2183
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of RhD immune globulin is classified as a form of التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). This specific immunoprophylactic intervention provides immediate, short‑term protection by supplying pre‑formed antibodies, thereby preventing Rh sensitization in Rh‑negative patients exposed to Rh‑positive blood. As a passive immunization strategy, the injection of RhD immune globulin exemplifies how targeted antibody administration can be used to avert alloimmunization and its associated complications in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.content: Finally, **Other puncture of artery** is placed under the parent category **Puncture of vessel**, reflecting its nature as a vascular access technique. The overarching class **Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations** encompasses **Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases**, **human vaccination**, and the subset **التمنيع السلبي** (passive immunity), which denotes the transfer of pre‑formed antibodies for immediate, short‑term protection without active immune response generation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1591
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease encompasses human vaccination as a specific form of immunization, indicating that vaccines administered to humans can be used to prevent or modulate autoimmune conditions. Human vaccination is situated within the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reflecting its role as a preventive intervention designed to protect individuals from infectious agents. Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions as the overarching classification that groups together all preventive vaccine strategies, including human vaccination, and thereby provides the hierarchical framework linking general preventive immunization concepts to the more specific application of immunization for autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2828
retrieved_docs.content: Immunological adjuvancia (immunological adjuvant) refers to agents that are employed to boost immune responses, and they can be formulated in diverse ways. One formulation category includes drug combinations that contain amylase, phosphates, and proteases, which together act as an immunological adjuvant by providing enzymatic and mineral components that modulate antigen presentation and immune activation. Another type of immunological adjuvant involves the steroid precursor dehydroepiandrosteron (dehydroepiandrosterone), which can function as an immunomodulatory substance enhancing immune function. Thus, both amylase‑phosphate‑protease drug combinations and dehydroepiandrosteron are recognized as specific instances of immunological adjuvants, illustrating the breadth of compounds that can serve this supportive role in immunotherapy and vaccine strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2235
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of RhD immune globulin is classified as a form of التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Within the broader framework of human vaccination, التمنيع السلبي represents a child category, indicating that passive immunization is considered a subset of vaccination strategies applied to humans. Consequently, the administration of RhD immune globulin exemplifies how passive immunization techniques are incorporated into human vaccination programs to provide immediate, short‑term protection, particularly in contexts such as preventing hemolytic disease of the newborn. This relationship underscores the role of passive immunization as an integral component of comprehensive vaccination approaches in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.content: Thyreoglobulin and omalizumab are recognized as permissible qualifiers in the context of ultrastructure, meaning that both of these agents may be used to label or characterize ultrastructural features in microscopic examinations. This allowance indicates that researchers and clinicians can employ thyreoglobulin, a thyroid hormone precursor protein, and omalizumab, an anti‑IgE monoclonal antibody, as specific markers when assessing cellular and subcellular architecture, thereby enhancing the precision of ultrastructural analysis.
